notes: Run 2026-07-07 (category 1 of 2). Active count was 59 — thinnest non-geography eligible category. Under-weight tier: $200 (7 active vs avg 11.8). All other non-geography categories had open draft PRs; PR #106 (07-02) covers religion_mythology_philosophy + pop_culture; answers from that PR avoided. Pack weighted toward $200 clues and gaps in Roman & Norse Mythology (4 active), World Mythology (3), Philosophical Schools (3), Religious Texts & Terms (3), Symbols/Rituals (2). Fresh docs fetched from Wikipedia REST API for Jupiter, Freyja, Valhalla, Ragnarök, Talmud, Confucius, Empiricism, Ganesha, Zoroastrianism, Janus, Eris, Mithraism, Diwali.

questions.clue: Herald of the gods and guide of souls into the afterlife, this Greek deity — aided by his winged sandals — served as messenger between Olympus and the mortal world; his Roman counterpart was Mercury.

citations.title: Hermes
citations.snippet: Hermes is an Olympian deity in ancient Greek religion and mythology considered the herald of the gods. He is also widely considered the protector of human heralds, travelers, thieves, merchants, and orators. He is able to move quickly and f

questions.clue: Observed across South Asia each autumn, this Hindu festival symbolizes the spiritual victory of light over darkness and good over evil, and is also marked in adapted forms by Jains and Sikhs.

citations.title: Diwali
citations.snippet: Dipavali, commonly known as Diwali, is the Hindu festival of lights, with variations celebrated in other Indian religions such as Jainism and Sikhism. It symbolises the spiritual victory of Dharma over Adharma, light over darkness, good ove

questions.clue: Named in Genesis 1–5 as the first human, this biblical figure is recognized across Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and the Baháʼí Faith as the ancestor of all humankind.

citations.title: Adam
citations.snippet: Adam is the name given in Genesis 1–5 to the first human. Adam is the first human being aware of God, and features as such in various belief systems.

questions.clue: This Norse goddess of love, beauty, fertility, and war possesses a cloak of falcon feathers for shapeshifting and the legendary necklace Brísingamen; she presides over Fólkvangr, receiving half of all warriors slain in battle.

citations.title: Freyja
citations.snippet: In Norse mythology, Freyja is a goddess associated with love, beauty, fertility, war, gold, and seiðr. Freyja is the owner of the necklace Brísingamen, rides a chariot pulled by two cats, is accompanied by the boar Hildisvíni, and possesses

questions.clue: Born Kong Qiu, this Chinese philosopher of the Spring and Autumn period taught that personal morality and harmonious social relationships form the foundation of a well-ordered society; his sayings were collected in the Analects.

citations.title: Confucius
citations.snippet: Confucius, born Kong Qiu, was a Chinese philosopher of the Spring and Autumn period who is traditionally considered the paragon of Chinese sages. Much of the shared cultural heritage of the Sinosphere originates in the philosophy and teachi

questions.clue: Second in authority only to the Hebrew Bible within Rabbinic Judaism, this central text is the primary source of Jewish law and theology, comprising the Mishnah and its interpretive commentary, the Gemara.

citations.title: Talmud
citations.snippet: The Talmud is the central text of Rabbinic Judaism, and second in authority only to the Hebrew Bible, the first five books of which form the Torah along with the texts in Nevi'im (Prophets) and Ketuvim (Writings). It is a primary source of

questions.clue: Located in Asgard and presided over by Odin, this majestic Norse hall receives half of those warriors chosen from the battlefield by the Valkyries to feast and train for the final cosmic battle.

citations.title: Valhalla
citations.snippet: In Norse mythology, Valhalla is described as a majestic hall located in Asgard and presided over by the god Odin. There were five possible realms the soul could travel to after death. The first was Fólkvangr, ruled by the goddess Freyja. Th

questions.clue: This foretold Norse cataclysm encompasses a great final battle in which Odin, Thor, and other major deities perish, the world burns and sinks beneath the sea — only to rise again, cleansed and fertile.

citations.title: Ragnarök
citations.snippet: In Norse mythology, Ragnarök is a foretold series of impending events, including a great battle in which numerous great Norse mythological figures will perish ; it will entail a catastrophic series of natural disasters, including the burnin

questions.clue: This philosophical tradition holds that genuine knowledge arises primarily from sensory experience and observation rather than innate ideas or pure reason; it stands alongside rationalism and skepticism as one of the major schools in epistemology.

citations.title: Empiricism
citations.snippet: In philosophy, empiricism is an epistemological view which holds that true knowledge or justification comes either only or primarily from sensory experience and empirical evidence. It is one of several competing views within epistemology, a

questions.clue: Recognized throughout India by his distinctive elephant head, this widely revered Hindu deity is associated with new beginnings and the removal of obstacles, and his veneration extends to Jain and Buddhist communities as well.

citations.title: Ganesha
citations.snippet: Ganesha or Ganesh, also known as Ganapati, Vinayaka and Pillaiyar, is one of the best-known and most revered and worshipped deities in the Hindu pantheon and is the Supreme god in the Ganapatya sect. His depictions are found throughout Indi

questions.clue: Among the world's oldest organized faiths, this Iranian religion centers on the teachings of Zarathushtra and reveres an uncreated, all-wise supreme deity called Ahura Mazda.

citations.title: Zoroastrianism
citations.snippet: Zoroastrianism, also called Mazdayasna and Behdin, is an Iranian religion centred on the Avesta and the teachings of Zarathushtra Spitama, who is more commonly referred to by the Greek translation, Zoroaster. Among the world's oldest organi

questions.clue: Depicted with two faces simultaneously gazing forward and backward, this Roman deity presided over beginnings and endings, passages and transitions; his name was given to the first month of the Roman calendar year.

citations.title: Janus
citations.snippet: In ancient Roman religion and myth, Janus is the god of beginnings, gates, transitions, time, duality, doorways, passages, frames, and endings. He is usually depicted as having a double-sided head. The month of January is named for Janus (I

questions.clue: Born into slavery in Phrygia and later banished from Rome, this Greek Stoic philosopher settled in Nicopolis in northwestern Greece, where his teachings on inner freedom and accepting what lies beyond our control attracted students from across the empire.

citations.title: Epictetus
citations.snippet: Epictetus was a Greek Stoic philosopher. He was born into slavery at Hierapolis, Phrygia and lived in Rome until his banishment, after which he spent the rest of his life in Nicopolis in northwestern Greece.

questions.clue: At a divine wedding feast, this Greek goddess of strife cast a golden apple inscribed 'for the fairest' among the assembled deities, igniting a rivalry between Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite that culminated in the Judgement of Paris and the Trojan War.

citations.title: Eris (mythology)
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Eris is the goddess and personification of strife and discord, particularly in war, and in the Iliad. According to Hesiod she was the daughter of primordial Nyx (Night), and the mother of a long list of undesirable perso

questions.clue: Especially popular among the Imperial Roman army from the 1st to the 4th century AD, this secretive Roman mystery religion was inspired by an Iranian Zoroastrian deity yet developed its own distinctly Roman imagery and practice.

citations.title: Mithraism
citations.snippet: Mithraism, also known as the Mithraic mysteries or the Cult of Mithras, was a Roman mystery religion focused on the god Mithras. Although inspired by Iranian worship of the Zoroastrian divinity (yazata) Mithra, the Roman Mithras was linked
